data.title: Amazon: Strong Q3 May Fail To Keep The Stock Soaring
data.description:
Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) is set to release its earnings for the September quarter on October 31st, after the market closes. According to data compiled by Refinitiv, analysts (at midpoint consensus) project around $157.2 billion in revenue and $15.3 billion in operating profits, reflecting an 11% and 42% YoY increase, respectively. Considering the broader macro backdrop, which remains favorable for e-commerce and cloud computing growth, these estimates appear reasonable, and I expect Amazon to meet or even exceed projections. Looking for Q4 and early 2025 guidance, however, I see downside risk, as cost and competitive pressure drive margin risk in the retail and cloud segment, respectively. On valuation, Amazon’s stock is trading at approximately 33x EV/EBIT for 2025 estimates, suggesting a stretched entry point compared to the valuation multiples seen in other Mag 7 stocks, notably Google (GOOG)(GOOGL) and Meta (META). As such, I downgrade Amazon shares to "Hold" heading into this earnings report.

For context, Amazon stock has performed about in line with the broader market this year. Year-to-date, AMZN shares have risen by about 24%, while the S&P 500 (SP500) has increased by roughly 23%.

Margin Improvement In Retail Business Could Slow Meaningfully

Amazon's retail segment has shown notable efficiency improvements, particularly through the regionalization strategy implemented under CEO Andy Jassy. Indeed, during the period spanning from Q1 2022 to Q2 2024, Amazon managed to increase the operating margin in the North America segment from -2.3% to 5.6%, mostly as a consequence of reduced fulfillment and shipping costs​.

I don't think Amazon's Retail business can maintain a similar margin trajectory over the next few quarters, as the comparable basis is more difficult to improve upon and persistent labor cost pressures cannot be ignored, particularly within the core retail and logistics operations: For context, the company employs over 1.5 million people globally, and rising wages are a significant contributor to increasing operational expenses. Moreover, Amazon faces continued labor-related headwinds due to external pressures such as increased union activities and regulatory scrutiny. The FTC has been advocating for stricter labor regulations, and there’s an ongoing push to classify third-party delivery drivers as full employees rather than contractors. For example, in September 2024, Amazon raised wages for approximately 800,000 U.S. workers by $1.50 per hour, bringing the average hourly wage to around $22. Assuming that Amazon workers bill about 2,200 hours a year, the incremental costs for Amazon could add up to about $2.6 billion.

At the same time, I am not very bullish on Amazon's ability to offset its higher cost base with higher prices. Indeed, I point out that Amazon's reliance on low-priced essentials, alongside Prime Day and promotional events like Big Deal Days, has led to a rise in low-ASP products. While essential goods provide strong volume growth, the overall profitability is lower, which makes me doubt that Amazon can sustain high retail margins amid a discretionary spending slowdown​.

AWS: Revenue Upside Clouded By Weaker Margins

AWS is expected to see another acceleration in its revenue growth throughout the second half of 2024, supported by new workloads, increased AI monetization, and easing optimization trends. For Q3 2024, AWS revenue is projected to grow around 20% YoY, according to data collected by Refinitiv, marking the fifth consecutive quarter of acceleration.

Despite the optimistic revenue figures, AWS's market dominance is increasingly under threat: In the last few quarters, it has become clear that AWS facing intensified competition from Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud Platform, and Oracle. Notably, Microsoft’s integration of OpenAI’s large language models with Azure and its exclusive access to GPT models are giving it a competitive edge, which is particularly attractive to enterprise clients focused on AI applications​. Google, in turn, is leveraging its Gemini models and data tools like BigQuery, resonating well with customers that require extensive AI and machine learning capabilities​. At the same time, I am worried that Oracle’s recent progress with its cloud infrastructure is becoming a direct threat for AWS, as Oracle has seen success in retaining and expanding its cloud customer base by leveraging on the AI and database needs of its existing clientele​.

On a longer dated perspective, I am not only concerned about AWS market share, but also about the structural change toward multi-cloud strategies. Enterprises are increasingly wary of single-cloud dependencies, and a substantial portion of them are choosing to diversify their cloud investments. In this context, AWS still remains the primary provider in many multi-cloud arrangements, but the market share dynamics are shifting​, and I doubt that AWS can extract monopoly-like profitability in 2-3 years from now.

Advertising: A Bright Spot

The only clear bright spot I can see in Amazon's upcoming Q3 report is advertising. On the advertising macro backdrop, UBS research noted (emphasis mine):

Budget growth accelerates throughout 3Q24: Despite a slower start, ad budgets accelerated in Aug into Sep citing uplift from political, brand advertising recovery, and improved consumer sentiment; and while most verticals ramped through 3Q24, we note particular strength in auto and CPG. Meta/Instagram and Google/YouTube both benefited from political spend and optimization/efficiencies unlock from automation and a greater share of budgets moving through Performance Max and Advantage+. (Source: UBS research note dated 7th October: 3Q24 Online Advertising Preview - Rising Tide Lifts All Boats on the 2024 Outlook)

Amazon's advertising business is running at a growth rate of around 20% YoY, and I expect a 20% YoY growth also for Q3. That said, I also argue that the integration of generative AI into Amazon's advertising tools and the incremental contribution from Prime Video ads could drive upside risk to the 20% benchmark. As a potential downside risk, the integration of costly content rights like the NBA into its advertising portfolio could drive up costs, potentially impacting margins.

Valuation: Only ~5% Upside

Amazon stock is currently trading at 33x EV/EBIT for 2025 estimates. This is somewhat expensive, in my view: Investors should consider that the EV/EBIT range for the Mag 7 stocks trends closer to 25x to ~30x (Meta 23x, MSFT 25x, AAPL 28x, GOOG 17x, NVDA 40x, TSLA 93x).

To be more precise on valuation, I highlight that I have previously estimated Amazon's fair implied value at approximately $201 per share. And heading into Q3, I continue to view this estimate as reasonable, based on the enclosed assumptions:

For FY 2024, 2025, and 2026, I now anticipate EPS to be approximately $4.8, $6.2, and $7.8, respectively. These EPS estimates are about 10%-15% higher than consensus, primarily due to underestimations of Amazon's advertising and cloud momentum. Additionally, I maintain a 3.5% terminal growth rate post-2026 and have adjusted my cost of equity assumption down by 50 basis points to 8.5%, aligning it with the broader Magnificent Seven group. With these updated EPS projections, I calculate a fair implied share price for Amazon at $201.

Looking at my target valuation of $201, and comparing it to Amazon's current trading price of $190, I would argue that there is currently little valuation upside in AMZN shares (only ~5% according to my calculation).

Investor Takeaway

Amazon is scheduled to report its earnings for the September quarter on October 31st, after market close. Analysts, based on Refinitiv data, anticipate revenue around $157.2 billion and operating profits near $15.3 billion, reflecting 11% and 42% year-over-year growth, respectively. Given the favorable macro environment for e-commerce and cloud services, these estimates seem attainable, and I believe Amazon could match or exceed them. However, looking ahead to Q4 and early 2025, I see potential downside risks due to increasing cost and competitive pressures impacting margins in both retail and cloud segments. In terms of valuation, Amazon’s stock currently trades at roughly 33x EV/EBIT based on 2025 estimates. This appears relatively high compared to valuation multiples of other Mag 7 stocks, like Google and Meta. Therefore, I’m downgrading Amazon shares to a "Hold" ahead of this earnings announcement.

data.title: AMD's Ryzen 9000 X3D Chips May Reignite A Consumer CPU Upgrade Cycle
data.description:
Introduction

Advanced Micro Devices, Inc.'s (NASDAQ:AMD) Zen 5 Ryzen 9000 (non-X3D) chips have so far been received with reviews that essentially sum up to “meh.” The new CPUs show little to no gains over the previous 7000 series chips, albeit with improved power efficiency. This has caused most consumers to forgo upgrades, leading to disappointing sales of AMD's new chips. However, AMD has now confirmed on X that the X3D versions of the new chips are right around the corner, leading to some excitement that the X3D chips could see better performance improvements in gaming than their non-X3D 9000 counterparts.

Brief History of the X3D Chips

AMD originally started the X3D versions of its chips with the Ryzen 7 5800X3D chip launched in April 2022. The X3D CPUs improve gaming performance substantially due to their increased L3 Cache relative to their non-X3D counterparts. This has the effect of significantly improving gaming performance, but comes at the cost of productivity tasks such as video editing and rendering, large computational programs, or various other productivity related workloads. Thus, the target demographic for the X3D chips is mostly gamers, or productivity users that also game on the same machine.

Ryzen 9000X3D

With AMD's announcement on X, they confirmed that at least one version of the new X3D chips will be launching on November 7, 2024. Unfortunately, that's all the details we have for now. I would expect a launch event shortly before that launch date announcing more detailed specs and performance metrics for these chips. I would also expect more than one version based on core counts (like previous launches), although the launch timing of each chip may vary.

Speculators have suggested, based on one leak, that the new X3D parts are somewhere between 10-30% faster than their 7000X3D predecessors. This is a substantial increase from the non-X3D Zen 5 chips that mostly only saw a low single digit percentage gain to no gain whatsoever in most games, or even a small regression in a few titles. Furthermore, early rumors suggest that these X3D chips may be able to keep up in productivity tasks with the non-X3D versions for the first time ever. This would make the chips a no-brainer for users that both play games and perform productivity related workloads.

However, this remains speculative, and we don't know what testing methods were used in any leaks, so it is highly possible that actual results upon release are different. Consumers should consult multiple unbiased review sources before making a purchase decision.

What This Means For Investors

AMD's stock price has been largely driven by AI related news, which has yet to really make its way into the consumer desktop space. For now, the hype around AI mostly applies to enterprise level and datacenter GPUs. While I'm moderately optimistic about AMD's opportunities here, this is an entire discussion for another article.

Ultimately, should these 9000X3D chips show strong performance, it could re-ignite a new upgrade cycle for gamers wanting to upgrade to the latest and fastest chips. Many consumers are likely still on Zen 3 waiting for a more meaningful upgrade path. These new chips have the potential to provide that, particularly at the high end.

Then again, if the processors disappoint, much like their non-X3D Zen 5 chips did, then the chips are likely mostly dead on arrival, with the only viable consumers being those building a brand-new high-end PC from scratch. In this scenario, gamers are more likely to wait yet another generation before having a strong enough performance uplift to want to upgrade.

AMD has been successfully taking market share away from Intel (INTC) in desktop CPUs for several years now. This is the result of AMD innovating faster than Intel and bringing more compelling products to market. This all started with the original 1st-generation Zen processors back in 2017, and has continued with AMD hitting new market share highs as of Q1 2024.

Since Q1, Intel's CPUs have hit some major roadblocks. Thus, it's likely that AMD CPUs have gained even more share in Q2 '24 and Q3 '24. These problems are covered in depth in this Gamers Nexus video for those interested.

Going forward, Intel's upcoming Ultra 200S series processors (the naming scheme is changing) do show some early signs of improvement. Intel has directly boasted about power efficiency gains with this new generation. In some ways, the new Intel chips will “reset” Intel's branding and marketing strategy and set a new base to beat for future generations. Thus, I'd expect Intel to claw back a small amount of market share post-launch, unless AMD's X3D chips really hit it out of the park. There is room here for Intel at the lower end in particular, as AMD's lowest end Zen 5 chip is the Ryzen 5 9600X, priced at USD280. This is generally a bit more than someone buying a budget gaming PC would look to spend on the CPU.

AMD's Valuation: Pricing In Strong Execution

Assessing AMD's valuation is not particularly easy, given differences to peers in Intel and Nvidia (NVDA). Obviously, Nvidia has been winning the enterprise datacenter GPU market, leading to truly unbelievable margins. This makes something like a Price/Sales ratio somewhat irrelevant, given the difference in margins. AMD also competes in the datacenter GPU space, but has been unable to match Nvidia's dominance.

Interestingly, investors assessing forward P/Es would find AMD and Nvidia trading at similar forward P/Es of just under 40. This is likely a combination of an inevitable slowdown in growth for Nvidia (to be clear, I'm not saying no growth, just a YoY decrease as they lap some 200% or more growth rates going forward for the next few quarters), and a continued growth path for AMD. This is especially true if they can take more market share from Intel in CPUs, and begin to penetrate Nvidia's market share on the GPU side (something they have not yet been able to achieve to a meaningful degree). Of course, Intel's multiples look funny given the company's recent struggles and likely temporarily reduced profitability (making the multiples appear high when the market is simply pricing in a reversion to more normalized profits in future years after a year or two of reduced profitability).

Long story short, AMD needs to continue executing to justify its current valuation. Its valuation multiples currently sit near the middle of where they've traded in the last few years. This is close to peers, but is not low on an absolute basis. Thus, if AMD can show strong execution starting with the new X3D chips and demonstrate continued execution across a multi-year road map, there may be some upside in the short term for those multiples as investors increase optimism. However, if they cannot continue to push the boundaries of CPU technology and with the continued struggles in datacenter GPUs, then there could perhaps be some downside in these multiples.

Conclusion

AMD's upcoming X3D chips are AMD's chance to compete with both their previous generations of CPUs, and Intel's upcoming Ultra 200S series of processors. Both these product lines should launch in the next month or so, so investors should tune in to product reviews to stay up to date with who is offering consumers the best products. Should AMD's X3D chips show a substantially greater performance uplift than previous versions of these chips, then investors could be in for increased sales via a new upgrade cycle. If, however, the performance is disappointing, I would expect things to mostly remain status quo for now.

data.title: Euroseas Capitalizes On Strong Market, But Growth Is Likely Priced In
data.description:
Euroseas Ltd. (NASDAQ:ESEA) is a $300 million ocean transporter that specializes in refrigerated and dry containerized cargo. These include both perishable items as well as manufactured products. With its total fleet of 23 vessels, containing 7 intermediate vessels, and 16 smaller feeder vessels, the company makes an average of over $31,600 per day through each individual vessel.

Like most players in this space, ESEA has been benefitting from favorable market conditions as a result of disruptions in the Red Sea, and low water levels in the Panama Canal, causing substantially high daily freight rates. The company is strategically using its surge in operating cashflow to build up its capacity, and expand its operations. While I believe we can expect significant performance growth in the following years, I feel that the market has already priced in the company's prospects. Moreover, I also feel that the risk of overcapacity in the industry is a looming danger, and it prevents me from entering into the stock. For this reason, I rate the stock as a hold.

Performance Overview

On the performance side, Euroseas came out with significant growth. Between Q2 of 2023 and 2024, the company saw its average fleet size increase by over 18% from 17.95 to 21.26. Similarly, its average daily net revenue or Time Charter Equivalent (TCE) rate climbed from $30,151 to $31,639. As a result of this, during Q2, the company came in with a total revenue figure of $58.7 million, compared to the prior year Q2 total of $47.7 million.

Going by the per day metrics, the average daily operational cost for each vessel, excluding drydocking, decreased from $7,114 to $6,612. Drydocking, however, saw a surge from $249 to $819 for each vessel, daily. This is not particularly concerning as dry-docking time cycles vary, and this quarter the company had two vessels go through drydocking, compared to none in FY23Q2. Drydocking expenses incurred during Q2 last year were linked to an upcoming drydocking that did not take place until at least Q3.

Despite the increased drydocking cost during the quarter, ESEA had its operating income nearly triple from $2.5 million to $6.4 million. Its performance on the bottom line was also impressive, with a YoY jump exceeding 40% with basic EPS going from $4.17 to $5.89. This is explained by a number of non-primary gains such as an unrealized gain on derivatives of $988,000 compared to the prior year where the gain amounted only to $118,000. Similarly, interest and other financing costs during the year actually dropped from $2.05 million to $1.20 million in 2024. As a result of all these, compared to other expenses of $1.6 million last year, ESEA actually saw positive 'other income' this year of above $60,000.

The driving factor behind this strong growth can be attributed to a very favorable freight rate environment, which has pushed up time charter-rates to their highest, second only to the post-Covid boom seen due to the global supply chain imbalance.

The reason behind this price surge is linked to immense pressures on the industry, with not only disruptions seen in the Middle East, with Houthi attacks on passing vessels, but also due to historically low water levels in Panama, causing a slowdown and restrictions on ships being able to pass through the vital canal. As a result, monthly transits on both the Suez and Panama canals are simultaneously approaching a record low.

While I believe the situation on the Panama side is already showing signs of recovery, the situation in the Middle East may continue to cause shipping disruptions (perhaps to a greater extent if we consider a possible escalation) in the Red Sea.

Fleet-Related Updates

Ever since the post-Covid boom in 2021, the company has been performing in a high spot environment, and as such, it has been seeing substantial growth in its operating cash flows.

The company has been using these cash flow gains to fund investments in expanding its fleet size and total capacity, which, I believe is a smart move. While the market conditions are favorable, expanding capacity and fleet size would essentially cause the company's operations to grow, and position it better to generate larger operating cash flows, even in the case when market conditions are normalized.

The company has a total 23 vessels in its fleet, that make up a total 67,073 twenty-foot equivalent units (TEUs). These include three feeder vessels with 1800 TEU each, which were delivered in May, June, and July. The TCEs for these three feeders came up at $16,000/day, $22,000/day, and $24,250/day, respectively. All three have already commenced chartering.

It will also be adding two more feeders to its fleet, which will be delivered by Q1 of 2025, adding 8.8% in overall deadweight tonnage.

The coverage of its fleet has been very well managed, with about 95% of its fleet fixed for 2024 and approximately 44.5% already set in for 2025. This will improve as charter contracts are renewed, and new ones initiated. Overall, the company follows a good chartering strategy that factors in the wider shipping market cycle in order to maximize revenue:

Overall, I believe the company is following an optimal approach towards attaining efficiency, while simultaneously maximizing its revenue stream. As the wider market conditions continue to bring in favorable spot rates, the company has positioned itself well to maximize its takeoff in these conditions.

Valuation

In order to gauge the valuation of the company, I am going with a peer group analysis, that includes 20 players from the marine transportation industry, both large and small. It ranges from players with market capitalizations in the $30 million range to large players with over $30 billion in market cap. The idea is that regardless of size, the market is likely to be consistent in how it values a single dollar in revenue growth in the industry, compared to the company's wider growth prospects. For this reason, I have created a scatter graph which compares the companies' trailing PS ratio with its revenue growth rate for the upcoming year. As you can see, the relationship is as expected:

As we can see that ESEA is slightly below the trend line, which would imply a slight undervaluation, but its closeness to this line of the best fit indicates that this implication is not really definitive. Moreover, ESEA's 5-year average figure of this PS ratio is actually 1.21 compared to its present figure of 1.46. This means that the PS ratio is 20.7% higher than the average level it is known to have traded at in the last 5 years.

Moreover, the company's present dividend yield of 5.31%, is still a lot lower than its five-year average yield of 6.44%. In fact, Maersk (AMKBY), the $23 billion Danish shipping giant, offers a much better present yield of 8.74%.

Looking at all of these together, I believe ESEA is neither definitively undervalued, not overvalued, but is trading at a fair price, with its future prospects priced in already. For this reason, I rate the stock as a hold.

Risk

Euroseas, along with most other players in the marine transportation industry, is currently enjoying positive market conditions resulting in high freight rates. However, we must not be blindsided by the deeper challenges impacting the market, particularly that of overcapacity. I believe the extent of this issue is not being fully felt at present because of the disruptions seen in the Red Sea, and in the Panama Canal.

Currently, most shipments that had gone through the Suez Canal are routing across Africa through the Cape of Good Hope. The longer travel times across this channel necessitates more ships being sent out to deliver cargo in a timely manner. If shipping companies were to resume normal routes through the Red Sea, the company would be faced with the full extent of the issue. When the situation does clear out, I believe Euroseas may feel the pressure, and would likely struggle to maintain current profit margins. Larger players in the industry may fare better, smaller companies like ESEA may struggle when freight rates take a plunge due to these supply demand dynamics.

Investor Takeaway

Euroseas is currently going through some strong growth, both in its top and bottom-line performance. While a lot of this has to do with favorable market conditions, the company's execution of a sound chartering strategy to maximize revenue, and to use its surging cashflows to expand its capacity is commendable. However, I cannot recommend a buy rating on the stock, as its valuation multiples do not exactly scream buy, and its dividend yield is significantly lower than some of its peers, including the shipping giant, Maersk. Moreover, I also believe that overcapacity in the market is a looming risk, which could sneak up on ESEA in the upcoming years. I rate the stock as a hold.

data.title: ADX: 8% Distribution Rate Policy And Discount Widening Back Out
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

When we last touched on the Adams Diversified Equity Fund (NYSE:ADX), we highlighted the tender offer opportunity and the change in the distribution policy. Since then, the tender offer has been completed, which we took advantage of, to our great benefit. We were able to rebuy the position at a lower price and pocket some of the additional cash difference.

ADX Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: 0.60
  • Discount/Premium: -11.40%
  • Distribution Yield: minimum 8% managed distribution
  • Expense Ratio: 0.59%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $2.9 billion
  • Structure: Perpetual

ADX's investment objective is simply; "long-term capital appreciation." The investment policy is quite straightforward as well. They intend to meet this objective by "investing primarily in large U.S. companies." They also add that they "seek to deliver superior returns over time by investing in a broadly diversified equity portfolio. The Fund invests in a blend of high-quality, large-cap companies."

Post Tender Slump

The tender offer was for 10% of outstanding shares at 98% of NAV, and I participated fully in the opportunity. As usual, not all investors participated, and in this case, it was a significant number. When tendering 100% of an investor's position, nearly 30% was accepted.

As usual, since then, the discount has widened back out a bit. This is generally seen in larger offerings, and 10% isn't too significant, but it appeared significant enough to make a difference. With the discount widening, that pressured the fund's total returns during this period. The fund's total share price returns slipped further relative to its actual underlying portfolio, represented by the fund's total NAV returns.

I've also included the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) for some reference. In general, the market had experienced some volatility during this period, with its pullback in August before recovering and slipping a bit heading into September.

Saba Capital Management was the activist group behind the push for a tender offer and the change in distribution policy. They have also since been selling down their position but remain with a fairly hefty position. That said, I wouldn't anticipate them to stick around for the long term if they find a different opportunity to move into next.

However, given the change in the distribution policy, I had expected that usual widening to be more muted; so far, that has appeared to be the case as ADX hasn't revisited the type of deep discount it had tended to trade at regularly.

Over the last five years, the average discount was nearly 14%-which was quite similar to what we've observed over the last decade for this fund. Heading into Covid, there was a bit of attention given to the fund to narrow the discount, but it otherwise slumped back out to a deep discount once Covid hit, never to really return to that level again until more recently.

Minimum 8% Managed Distribution

For investors not familiar with ADX's previous distribution policy and the change earlier this year, here is a recap:

Under the new Policy, the Fund commits to distribute a minimum 8% annual rate of the Fund's average net asset value ("NAV") to shareholders beginning with the third quarter distribution expected to be paid at the end of August 2024. The Fund expects to distribute 2% of average NAV for each quarterly distribution, with the fourth quarter distribution expected to be the greater of 2% of average NAV or the amount needed to satisfy minimum distribution requirements of the Internal Revenue Code for regulated investment companies. Average NAV will be based on the average of the previous four quarter-end NAVs per share prior to each declaration date.

The fund had paid out $0.05 per quarter for several years and then would top it off with a large year-end special. With the new policy, it should smooth out the distribution more throughout the year, and we saw that with last quarter's $0.44 distribution. The next payout is set to be announced in early November, and this will be a particularly interesting one.

It could be interesting because a portion of the year was still under the old distribution policy of having those nickels paid. At this point, to reach the minimum 8% distribution policy this year, they would have to pay out a larger year-end special still.

The broader market has been strong and that includes ADX providing around a 21% total NAV return on a YTD basis. If they realize a significant portion of those underlying gains, we could be in for an even larger year-end than simply the minimum 8%.

That's how a fund like ADX that invests in low dividend payers will cover their distribution to investors through capital gains being realized. That isn't any different from other mainly equity-focused CEFs but is worth noting for potentially newer investors in this space.

In looking at the semi-annual report for the first half of the year, the fund could be set for quite a hefty payout as well. This is because the fund realized a large portion of capital gains already-~87% of what they realized all of 2023.

Of course, the caveat here is that they could have or can spend the second half of the year realizing losses to offset those sizeable gains.

Over the last five years, the average annual distribution rate actually worked out to 9.3%, easily exceeding the new minimum policy anyway.

2021 was a particularly strong year, and the performance so far in 2024 has been similar. On the other hand, 2023's results exceeded those of 2021's, and they ended up paying a lower amount. It all comes down to what they do in terms of realizing gains/losses.

For example, the average NAV based at quarter end of each of the prior four quarters comes to $21.66 currently. This will change when factoring in the actual year-end distribution because it should be using the October ending NAV, which is actually looking like it should be higher than last October's. With that, 8% of that average NAV works out to $1.7326-with $0.54 already paid out this year, that would leave $1.1926.

Despite a change in the distribution policy, the distribution tax classifications shouldn't change all that dramatically going forward. The fund largely saw long-term capital gains as the most significant source of its distribution, and that should continue to be expected. It just is getting delivered more evenly throughout the year rather than at the end.

Portfolio And Performance Comparison

The fund generally invests in an S&P 500 Index-like manner. That includes a significant weighting in the information technology sector.

The top ten accounts for over 41% of the fund, with the mega-cap tech names representing a particularly large presence in the fund.

With a simple approach of mirroring the broader U.S. market, the fund fortunately comes with a much lower relative expense ratio when compared to other CEFs. Of course, the main argument would be that an S&P 500 Index ETF rather than a CEF is going to be even cheaper.

That's quite true but ADX has been able to outperform the index as well on an annualized basis in most periods based on the market price. This would be thanks to seeing its discount narrow this year. Further discount narrowing could push this even further.

Conclusion

With the fund's discount widening back out, it makes it an interesting consideration once again. It hasn't returned back to the deep discounts we've seen previously. Under normal market conditions, I wouldn't anticipate that happening, given the distribution policy change to make it a relatively more appealing fund.

That said, the broader equity market appears to be pretty richly valued in the U.S., and that could give some investors pause before wanting to invest too aggressively. Utilizing a dollar-cost average approach could be the more cautious and appropriate way to build a potential position if one is looking to add this fund to their roster.

data.title: SBA Communications Corporation: A Capital Structure Supporting Dividend Growth (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
Since I last covered SBAC Communications Corporation (NASDAQ:SBAC) back in May 2023 where I had a Hold position based on the need for a business reinvention, it suffered from a 19% downside as of October last year proving me right. Subsequently, it started trending higher and was trading at $247.47 at the time of writing as shown below.

Now, since my last coverage, there have been two major changes, firstly, CFO Brendan Cavanagh was promoted to CEO and second, the Federal Reserve is easing monetary policy, which impacts cell tower REITs considerably given the capital intensity of investments and the dividends they pay.

With the third quarter financial of 2024 (Q3) results likely to be announced on October 28, this thesis aims to show that SBAC is a buy based on the suitability of its capital structure to the new interest rate environment, and this after considering the risks. To start with, two growth drivers can help offset the problem of revenue stagnation.

Growth Drivers to Offset Temporary Weakness

First, coming back to last year's thesis, I highlighted the need to focus more on value-added services at the cell tower location instead of focusing merely on footprint expansion. Well, as part of his strategy to capitalize on the growth of mobile network usage, the new CEO has indeed shown a strong desire to address the demand for value-added services like FWA (Fixed Wireless Access), which is similar to the office WIFI but is built on top of a cellular network and is available remotely.

Furthermore, FWA is expanding as high-speed mobile penetration increases and constitutes an additional load on top of the existing cellular coverage, something which has been transformed into an opportunity by towerCos namely by leasing additional sites for MNOs (mobile network operators) to deploy their radio antennae. Also, given FWA consumes at least 20 times more data than conventional mobile traffic, this presents significant opportunities given that all three major carriers, namely AT&T (T), Verizon (VZ), and T-Mobile (TMUS) are now involved.

In addition, the REIT expects increased spending by MNOs to honor their 5G coverage commitments. These investments should get an impetus by borrowing costs coming down and after the 50 basis points cut in September, analysts at J.P Morgan (JPM) expect the same during the next meeting in November. Along the same lines, according to a report by Omdia covering global 5G adoption, North America has been leading with 32% of all wireless cellular connections this year.

However, SBAC has not fully experienced these opportunities as it has suffered from cancellations related to T-Mobile's acquisition of Sprint in 2020 signifying churn increased during the last three years. In addition, it also faced a reduction in 5G spending last year due to high inflation driving up costs for MNOs (or towerCos' customers) leading them to reduce capex. Because of these two factors, revenue growth has been modest before stagnating in FY-2023 as shown in the chart below, but worst, analysts estimate a decline this year as shown in red.

Eventually, growth is expected to resume in FY-2025. Therefore, the period of low growth may be temporary and, in the meantime, it is important to assess whether it has the financial ability to continue investing to meet carrier demand relating to FWA and continue to pay dividends.

Comparing the Capital Structure with Peers

To this end, its balance sheet held $220.5 million in cash and equivalents versus $14.3 billion of debt at the end of Q2. It seems highly leveraged with a net debt to adjusted EBITDA leverage ratio of 6.4 times compared to 4.8x for American Tower (AMT) and 5.5x for Crown Castle (CCI). However, it is important to go deeper into the capital structure.

In this case, as shown in the chart below both CCI and AMT have a higher debt-to-equity ratio, compared to -2.3 for SBAC, showing a lower equity base tending to rely heavily on debt.

Then, its advantage is lower rates reduce its debt servicing costs.

In this case, while most (97%) of its debt is at a fixed rate like the $620 million ABS Tower Securities maturing this month, there is floating rate debt too. Thus, interest payments coming down opens the possibility of refinancing at lower costs. This means that its strategy to rely mainly on debt to finance growth will benefit, but subject to rates continuing to be lowered and generating enough returns on investment to offset the cost of capital.

Looking into the rear mirror, this has been the case as shown in the deep blue chart below when SBAC sharply increased its ROIC in 2021 when interest rates were around 0.6%. Tellingly, the acceleration in SBAC's returns distinguishes it from its two peers in the cell tower business which remains highly capital-intensive and highlights its ability to profit from lower rates.

This is the reason why I believe that going ahead into 2025, it is the best telecom tower REIT to bet on.

Lower Rates Benefit Profitability and FFO as FWA Expands

Another reason that favors SBAC is because it did not sell hard tower assets like American Tower (AMT) or Crown Castle (CCI) which is reportedly in talks to sell its fiber and small cell business. In my opinion, SBAC's relative "stability" in the face of adverse market conditions, namely both higher rates and lower site development activities due to the T-Mobile consolidation, now provides it more leeway to expand its infrastructure to meet the demands of mobile data consumption, which is expected to grow at 27.6% CAGR from 2023 to 2030. Thus, it deserves better.

Next, for valuing the stock, the Seeking Alpha Quant system flags it as a strong sell. Looking deeper, this is based on high valuations relative to the REIT sector median and an unfavorable revision grade. On the other hand, its growth, profitability, and momentum metrics are all green. Now while the momentum metric has indirectly captured the effects of lower interest rates by being responsive to associated market movements, it has not fundamentally considered the effects of monetary policy easing on profitability, a metric for which SBAC already boasts a superior grade as shown below.

For a REIT, this is best measured using the FFO metric as it takes into account noncash elements like depreciation/amortization on tower assets and gains/losses on property sales. In this case, the FFO to gross margin which is already above the sector median by 7% stands to benefit from rate cuts, and to make my point, FFO per share increased by roughly 40% from 2021 to around March 2022 as shown below, after the Fed had loosened monetary conditions following the Covid freeze.

Deserves Better but faces Rate sensitivity Risks and Dividend Growth is a Plus

Now, given that interest rates remain at 5% which is still elevated relative to below 1% in 2021, I assume that SBAC can grow its FFO by 10%, or one-quarter of the 2021-2022 period as the Fed gradually cut rates to approach the 3.5% level by end-2025. This in turn translates into a target of $272.2 (247.5 x 1.1) based on the current share price of $247.5.

To support this optimism, analysts also estimate the company to post an FFO gain in FY-25 or an improvement over this year. Their estimate is for a 2.83% YoY growth which I believe is on the low side as it may not be taking into consideration the growth of the FWA market which is expected to grow by a CAGR of 6.98% from 2024 to 2028. Thus, adding 6.98% to 2.83% totals 9.81%, or roughly the 10% target I identified earlier.

Pursuing on a cautionary tone, there are risks the bright picture I have painted above be tainted by inflation again raising its ugly head and preventing the U.S Central Bank from cutting rates as intended. Here, I have in mind the Middle East conflict escalating and causing oil prices to rise or a Republican-controlled White House potentially imposing 60% tariffs on all Chinese-made products resulting in elevated goods prices. Such events would induce a large degree of volatility to SBAC’s stock which has advanced by around 9% since August 24 when the Fed’s Chairman Mr. Powell reiterated that interest rates would be cut. This upside could be reversed in case the conflict degenerates.

Still, in a period of uncertainty, you want to be invested in a stock that pays dividends. In this connection, SBAC currently pays the lowest dividends out of the three as tabled below, but, the pace at which they have grown, or 59% CAGR during the last five years is simply astounding. On top, it has room to expand its dividends since its payout ratio is the lowest.

Therefore, this represents a worthwhile investment, but for those wanting more clarity before investing, there are two important factors to look for during the forthcoming earnings call.

What to Look for during Q3's Earnings Call

Noteworthily, Q3 is the first earnings call after the 50 basis rate cut of September, and, firstly, it is important to assess the FFO guidance for FY-2024, which has already been revised upward two times during the last three months as shown below. I believe that a further upgrade remains possible as earlier management comments have hinted about the REIT "coming off of a period of reduced network investment by their largest domestic customers". Also, during the second quarter earnings call, the company did not reduce expectations for gross profit contribution as it continues to secure higher margin work, which is normally associated with value-added services at the cell tower.

On the other hand, things are more muted for revenues as due to lower development activities, less construction volume for 2024 was envisaged leading to the outlook being lowered by $10 million. Here, there is the longer-term rationale based on the 5G mid-band spectrum where coverage remains at around 50%, implying significant growth potential, especially for FY-2025 as the effects of the T-Mobile consolidation taper off and there is a pickup in 5G adoption as mentioned earlier, especially in the North American market, and as FWA deployments lead to more leasing activities.

In conclusion, this thesis has a buy position based both on lower rates and higher site development business from FWA and 5G. In addition, SBAC's ability to have navigated a period where the cost of capital was high without selling assets positions it to prosper as monetary conditions improve and one of its options is to grow faster through asset acquisition.

data.title: Ford's Q3 Earnings: +38% PHEV Sales Increase And Declining EBIT
data.description:
Among the Detroit Big Three, only GM seems to be sailing through a difficult market with some degree of success. On the other hand, Stellantis (STLA) and Ford (NYSE:F) (NEOE:F:CA) have their headaches to face. After all, S&P Global has recently warned about the tough road ahead of the auto industry due to credit deterioration and weaker-than-expected sales.

As we approach Ford's Q3 earnings report, some pieces of the puzzle we need to solve when shaping a preview is already available. In particular, in addition to Ford's guidance at the end of Q2, we already know Ford's Q3 sales.

At the end of Q2, Ford updated its FCF guidance for FY2024, increasing it by $1B to $8.5B and targeting a return between 40% and 50% of its FCF to shareholders. Thanks to a strong balance sheet with $27B in cash and $45 in available liquidity, Ford's returns seem sustainable. Net of debt, Ford holds $6.2B, which is decent.

However, at the end of Q2, Ford's EBIT was down by 27% YoY to $2.8B, with an EBIT margin of 5.8%. In particular, the company's electric vehicle business is still unprofitable and reported a negative $1.1B in EBIT in just one quarter. For the first half, EBIT was negative by $2.5B, which is quite a hit. Ford, on the other hand, has a highly profitable business in the pro segment, where its EBIT margins hover around 16%. In other words, Pro is financing EVs. This was the focus of a topic Ford Pro CFO Navin Kumar was asked by Adam Jones at Morgan Stanley's 12th Annual Laguna Conference:

Year-to-date, Ford Pro's first-half EBIT is $5.6 billion. Ford's total company EBIT is $5.5 billion. Ford Pro, again, I'm brilliant at math, is over 100% of total company EBIT. A common criticism from investors, including many in this room, is that Ford Pro generates all the damn cash, all the cash of the company and that, that money is going to pay for loss-making EVs and warranty costs. Is that fair?

The question was left unanswered.

Ford reported a 4% retail sales increase in the third quarter, which contrasts with flat sales in the U.S. market. Hybrid vehicles are being favored, with a 38% YoY increase to 48,101 units, while EVs increased only by 12% YoY. However, Ford sold 67,689 EVs through September and claims to be second only to Tesla in the U.S.

Ford's leadership in trucks, with its F-series sales outselling the Chevrolet Silverado by 138,000 units and the Ram by a whopping 282,000 trucks. Moreover, F-150 hybrid sales were up 64% YoY to 20,129 units, topping even the Ford Maverick, whose sales rose 22% to 16,561.

ICE vehicles, on the other hand, saw their sales decrease by over 12,000 units to 432,429 in the quarter, a 2.8% YoY decline. YtD, ICE sales are down 1.8%, but total vehicle sales are up 2.7% to 1,548,172 units. In a softening market, this is a very good result. Ford has three main types of vehicles: SUVs, trucks, and cars. Their sales are all up YtD, but in Q3, SUVs reported a 5.6% decline and car sales were down 8.2%. However, cars are a minor segment, with only 9,000 units sold in the quarter. It is the segment that reports the Mustang sales. Trucks take the lion's shares with 292,010 vehicles of the 504,039 Ford sold in the third quarter (58% of the mix). Lincoln brand seems recovering and reported a 26% YoY increase in sales, thanks to the Nautilus and the Aviator.

Compared to Ford's sales report in Q3 2023, inventory is up from 424,400 units to 587,700. This confirms the trend we had read in Ford's quarterly reports, with the TTM inventory up to $17.18B vs. $15.65B at the end of FY2023. What we see from the Q3 sales report is a 38.5% YoY increase in units, and I am expecting the earnings call to address this trend with an explanation of what the company plans on doing to keep it under control. While Ford Pro has the margins to discount a bit, the rest of Ford can't afford more discounts if it wants to run at a profit.

While we read the October sales report, we should not, in any case, overlook an issue that eroded Ford's profitability in Q2 and might probably have an impact on Q3 as well: increased warranty costs associated with its EV division. Just to give an idea, the word "warranty" occurred 26 times in the last earnings call, and Ford's President and CEO Jim Farley could only say the company is trying to improve its quality but still anticipates higher warranty costs down the road for this fiscal year. In an environment where a pricing war seems to be ramping up rapidly with the aggressive moves of Chinese OEMs, it is not a great thing to have this kind of cost.

Now, Ford's balance sheet is in good shape, with $18.7B in industrial debt and over $26B in cash and available liquidity. But if its profits keep being eroded, and we see its EBITDA plunge below $6B we would have its industrial debt/EBITDA ratio spike up above 3x, which is not a good sign. In this case, we might be seeing Ford reconsider once again a dividend cut. After all, Ford is indeed guiding for FCF of $8.5B, but it also spends around $8B in R&D.

Ford Q3 Earnings Preview

So, the picture is mixed. Ford sales seem to be holding up quite well, but we have its profitability under scrutiny. After all, for the last 3 months, we have 18 analysts releasing an EPS down revision while only one increased its estimates. At the same time, Ford's revenue has 8 up revisions and 4 down revisions. This means analysts are mainly expecting a deteriorating profitability.

In Q3, Ford should report around $42B in revenue, which would be close to a 2% YoY growth. I believe we might actually see Ford top this threshold, given the strong performance of Ford Pro. However, I think the current EPS estimate of $0.48 for Q3 might be a bit too optimistic since it represents a 23.5% YoY growth.

After all, Ford's net income margin is around 2-3%. Therefore, we could expect around $1B in net income. Considering Ford has 4B shares outstanding, we would have Ford's Q3 EPS around $0.26, which would mean flat growth YoY. Now, it is true that Ford is guiding for $2B in manufacturing savings this year, thanks to lower inflation and cheaper raw material prices. So, it would not be absurd to assume Ford could report a 4% net income margin, which would make Ford report $1.68B in net income. If we consider that there might be a probable headwind coming from warranty costs, let's place our estimate at $1.5B in Q3 net income. In this case, our EPS estimate jumps up to $0.38. I think this is a more reasonable forecast than the current consensus. What does this mean? That the FY EPS estimate might fall short of the $1.87 analysts are expecting, which puts Ford at a 6 fwd PE. The fwd PE might actually be closer to 6.5. Now, although this may seem dirt cheap, it is not. Remember, we are talking about an automaker. A cheap PE in this industry is a 2 or a 3. Above a 6 we are somewhat talking about a premium. Of course, Ford Pro is a strong business. But, as we have seen, it is being diluted by Ford's other two business lines.

Not by chance, Ford is one of the few automakers not earning an A+ as its Quant Factor Valuation Grade. GM, which is currently performing better, trades at a fwd PE just above a 5. The same is true with the more troubled Stellantis. Volkswagen trades at a fwd PE of 3. This means Ford is twice as expensive as Volkswagen right now. But I find this valuation hard to justify.

As a result, although I really like the Ford Pro business, I don't see great value right now in this stock. These earnings, in addition, may see the stock go through some volatility since investors might get nervous if Ford's warranty issues keep being reported. Given the current environment and the available data, I would stay neutral. Therefore, I rate the stock as a hold heading into earnings.
